original_abstract: Gas separations using porous materials are of immense economic significance, yet they present a distinct challenge in materials design: the need to precisely tune adsorbent properties to achieve high selectivity. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have emerged as a versatile class of porous materials, offering nearly limitless architectural possibilities for engineering specific functionalities. Given the vast landscape of potential framework structures, quantum mechanical simulations serve as an essential tool for identifying and prioritizing the most promising materials before synthesis. In this work, we use these computational methods to predict a novel MOF tailored for the separation of dinitrogen from methane—a notoriously difficult process critical for the purification of natural gas. We demonstrate that the incorporation of open V(II) sites into the framework results in a significantly higher enthalpy of adsorption for dinitrogen compared to methane. This selectivity is driven by strong, specific back-bonding interactions with dinitrogen that are absent in the case of methane, providing a robust mechanism for efficient gas separation.
humanized_abstract: Gas separations using porous materials are of great economic importance. Designing adsorbents that have the desired properties for high selectivity is a materials design challenge that is not well understood. MOFs offer the possibility of almost unlimited architectural design for functional materials. Based on the enormous possibilities of MOF design, quantum mechanical calculations can serve as a guide to focus materials design on structures that will give the desired properties before they are synthesized. Here, we use computational chemistry methods to demonstrate the design of a MOF material that has the capability of separating dinitrogen (N2) from methane (CH4) — one of the hardest separations to achieve in particular for natural gas processing. By incorporating open V(II) sites in the MOF structure, an increased enthalpy for N2 adsorption compared to CH4 is achieved. This selectivity of the two gases is a result of a specific back-bonding of the V(II) centres with N2, which is not present for CH4.

This selectivity of the two gases is a result of a specific back-bonding of the V(II) centres with N2, which is not present for CH4.
